text: The laparoscopic view reveals a large inflammatory mass involving the cecum, small bowel, and appendices epiploicae. The cecum appears inflamed and thickened, forming a firm mass. Adhesiolysis is performed to identify the appendix, with visible peritoneal flakes indicating a possible contained perforation.

The laparoscopic view reveals a large inflammatory mass involving the cecum, small bowel, and appendices epiploicae. The surgeon performs meticulous adhesiolysis to identify the appendix, which is visible at its tip. The cecum appears firm and thickened, indicating localized peritonitis. Peritoneal flakes are present, suggesting a contained perforation.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a blunt grasper or suction tip to separate inflamed tissues, which appear thick and difficult to handle due to inflammation. Minimal handling is advised to prevent oozing. The appendix epiploicae is adhered to surrounding structures, and meticulous dissection is used to peel off fibrinolytic adhesions, revealing pus from a perforation likely at the tip or distal one-third of the appendix.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a blunt grasper or suction tip to separate inflamed tissues, which appear thick and difficult to handle. The appendix epiploicae is adhered to surrounding structures, and meticulous dissection is used to peel away these adhesions. Pus becomes visible, indicating a perforation likely in the distal one-third of the appendix.

The surgeon reveals a perforation at the base of the appendix, with the surrounding cecum appearing gangrenous and sloughed. The area of perforation is visible, with pus actively oozing from the affected tissues, indicating an ongoing perforation.

The surgeon reveals the perforation at the base of the appendix, showing gangrenous and sloughed tissue extending to the cecum wall. Gentle dissection is performed to avoid trauma, and pressure applied to the cecum causes pus to emerge from the perforated area, indicating active perforation.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum on the inferior wall, gradually removing sloughed tissue to identify the extent of diseased tissue for excision. Conventional suturing is considered due to the friable and inflamed nature of the tissue. The surgical field reveals a large perforation, with manipulation occurring between the cecum and the inferior mesentery.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum on the inferior wall, gradually sucking off sloughed tissue and manipulating the area to identify the extent of diseased tissue for excision. A large perforation is visible, with thick, fibrotic adhesions to the abdominal wall. Gauze is used to manage oozing and improve visibility.

The surgeon observes a sloughed-out tissue mass resembling a phlegmon in the appendix, with thick fibrotic adhesions to the abdominal wall. Gauze is used to manage oozing and improve visibility. The cecum is mobilized further, applying traction to identify avascular planes between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia, similar to the approach in a right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the cecum, applying traction to identify avascular planes between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia. Small oozes are mopped with gauze to brighten the image. Sharp dissection is performed tissue by tissue, using ultrasonic shears to minimize bleeding. The sloughed tissue is gently removed, and care is taken to avoid retroperitoneal injury. The appendix is incised, staying close to the mesentery to protect vessels. The appendicular artery is coagulated using bipolar forceps.

The surgeon uses gauze to mop small oozes, brightening the surgical field. Sharp dissection is performed on small tissue segments, avoiding large blocks, to ensure precision. The use of ultrasonic shears helps manage small thrombosed vessels and minimizes bleeding in inflamed tissues.

The surgeon gently removes sloughed tissue to identify the appendix, making careful incisions while avoiding retroperitoneal structures. The dissection is performed close to the appendix to prevent injury to mesenteric vessels. Edematous tissues produce smoke, but the surgeon continues to identify and cut tissue planes sharply, using bipolar cautery to coagulate the appendicular artery.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to coagulate a vessel, effectively controlling bleeding in the surgical field. Gauze is then used to mop up the area, providing traction and retraction without traumatizing inflamed tissue, while maintaining pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to coagulate a vessel, achieving hemostasis and controlling bleeding in the inflamed tissue. Gauze is used to mop the area, providing traction and retraction without traumatizing the tissue. The surgical field is maintained with an optical trocar in the umbilicus and working ports at the suprapubic level and right lumbar region, facilitating manipulation and suturing of the phlegmon mass.

The surgeon manipulates the phlegmon mass using assisting hands, with an optical trocar placed at the umbilicus and working ports at the suprapubic level and right lumbar region. Meticulous removal of tissues around the appendix is performed, taking care to avoid thermal injury to the cecal wall. Fatty tissues are gradually removed, and a bipolar instrument is used to coagulate any oozing tissues.

The surgeon meticulously dissects and releases the appendix, taking care to avoid thermal injury to the cecal wall. Fibrotic adhesions are lysed using bipolar coagulation to free the appendix for appendectomy. The base of the appendix is exposed, revealing gangrenous and perforated areas at the junction with the cecum. Small fatty strands are dissected and divided sharply.

The surgeon lyses remaining fibrotic tissue to free the appendix for appendectomy. The appendix is dissected in a cylindrical manner, revealing the junction with the cecum. The gangrenous and perforated areas are visible at the base of the appendix. Traction is applied to inspect the tissues, and sloughed tissue at the cecum level is incised, avoiding close proximity to the cecal wall to assess the extent of infection and unhealthy tissue.

The surgeon applies traction to expose the sloughed tissue at the cecum level, carefully incising it to assess the extent of infection and unhealthy tissue. The ultrasonic shears are used to completely divide the appendix, as an endo-loop is not feasible. The surgeon uses suction to evaluate the depth of sloughing into the cecum, revealing a tunneled area with pus, while ensuring the surrounding cecal wall appears healthy. The goal is to remove sloughed tissue to determine the level of vascularity for potential closure.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to completely divide the appendix, carefully excising it to minimize damage to the cecum. The surrounding tissue appears sloughed and unhealthy, prompting the use of suction and a dramatic instrument to assess the depth of sloughing into the cecum. The surgeon identifies the level of vascularity and healthiness of the tissue for closure, noting pus being extracted from the cecum.

The surgeon uses suction to remove pus from the cecum, revealing healthier tissue beneath. The edges of the sloughed tissue are refashioned using ultrasonic shears, ensuring only healthy tissue remains. The last of the gangrenous tissue is excised and kept with the specimen to prevent infection. The cecum's wall appears healthy, with the collection being suctioned out.

The surgeon removes pus from the cecum using suction, revealing healthier tissue. The ultrasonic shears are used to excise remaining gangrenous and sloughed tissue, ensuring only healthy edges remain. The suction apparatus is palpated to confirm the small opening into the cecum, which is now clear of unhealthy tissue. The surgeon prepares to suture the cecal opening using 2-0 Vicryl, after mobilizing the cecum inferiorly for better access.

The surgeon has removed all sloughed tissue, revealing healthy cecal walls with good vascularity. The suction apparatus is used to remove collected fluid and pus from the cecum. The surgeon prepares to suture the cecal opening using 2-0 Vicryl, taking thick bites for interrupted sutures to prevent cutting through the inflamed tissue. The needle is introduced through a 10 mm port, and the posterior layer is included in the suturing process.

The surgeon guides the needle through a ten mm port to perform interrupted sutures on the inflamed tissue. Thick bites are taken to prevent cutting through the friable tissue, ensuring a watertight closure of the opening. The needle is manipulated to achieve precise exit points, and knots are tightened to approximate the tissues without excessive pressure. The suturing process is completed with careful control of the needle's curvature to minimize trauma.

The surgeon performs a surgeon's throw followed by single throws to approximate the tissue edges without excessive pressure, preventing tissue cut-through. The tissues are brought close together, forming a clear dumbbell shape. The surgeon takes closely spaced bites for a watertight closure, preventing bowel contents from spilling into the abdominal cavity. The limbs of the sutures are kept small, with one slightly longer for manipulation. A full-thickness bite is taken, controlling the needle's exit point precisely to minimize tissue trauma.

The surgeon tightens the knot to approximate the tissues, ensuring a secure closure. The third bite is taken, controlling the needle's exit point to achieve precise placement. The needle is removed, and the knot is secured using a double throw followed by single throws, promoting wound healing without cutting through the tissues.

The surgeon removes the needle and knots the suture using a double throw followed by single throws to secure the closure. The surgical field is kept clean as the omentum is identified and pulled close to the cecum. A seromuscular bite is taken on the cecal wall edge and the omentum, performing a pexy to control potential leakage and localized peritonitis. The sutures are tightened to keep the omentum in place, securing the suture line.

The surgeon completes the final knot of the stitch, ensuring the surgical field remains clean. The omentum is identified and pulled close to the cecum, where a seromuscular bite is taken on the cecal wall near the suture line. A bite is then taken in the omentum to perform a pexy, securing the omentum to the suture line to minimize leakage and control peritonitis. The sutures are tightened to keep the omentum in place, aiding in localized healing.

The surgeon places additional omentum in the right iliac fossa to help control inflammation. The small bowel is examined from the terminal ileocecal junction to check for Meckel's diverticulum. An endobag is introduced into the abdomen, and the dissected appendix is placed inside to prevent spillage during removal. A drain is placed in the right iliac fossa and pelvis, and the procedure concludes with the closure of the ports using sutures.

The surgeon places additional omentum in the right iliac fossa to help control inflammation. The small bowel is examined from the terminal ileocecal junction to check for Meckel's diverticulum. An endobag is introduced into the abdomen, and the dissected appendix is placed inside to prevent spillage during removal. A drain is placed in the right iliac fossa and pelvis, and the procedure concludes with port closure using sutures.
